title: Neither US Exceptionalism Nor A European Boom
gist: Recent US tech stock volatility has revived the binary debate over whether US equities keep outperforming or whether the rest of the world is now poised to lead. Gavekal notes that the MSCI US/MSCI Europe total-return ratio has round-tripped back to its early-2022 level, cautioning against both a US-exceptionalism and a European-boom narrative.

insights.claim: Recent volatility in US technology stocks has reignited the debate over US exceptionalism, which from an equity-market perspective is often framed as a binary choice between continued US outperformance and the rest of the world taking the lead.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Positioning framed as all-or-nothing US-vs-world creates crowded trades on both sides; a middle path leaves both consensus views vulnerable.
insights.quote: Recent volatility in US technology stocks has reignited the debate over US exceptionalism.

insights.claim: The debate over Europe's equity outlook is framed in similarly binary terms as the US-exceptionalism debate, pitting continued underperformance against a poised European boom.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Investors betting on a decisive European breakout should note the framing itself may be a false binary.
insights.quote: The debate over Europe’s outlook is framed in similarly binary terms.

insights.claim: As of July 17, 2026, the ratio of the MSCI US index to the MSCI Europe index, on a total-return basis in US dollar terms, stood exactly at its early March 2022 level, implying no net US outperformance over that span.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A four-plus-year round trip in the relative ratio undercuts the case for durable US equity exceptionalism.
insights.quote: the ratio of the MSCI US index to the MSCI Europe index, measured on a total-return-basis in US dollar terms, stood exactly at its early March 2022 level

insights.claim: On an equally-weighted basis, the MSCI US to MSCI Europe ratio stood at its early October 2021 level as of July 17, 2026, suggesting US outperformance has been concentrated in the largest-cap names rather than broad-based.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The equal-weight round trip to late-2021 hints that mega-cap tech, not the broad market, drove apparent US leadership.
insights.quote: On an equally-weighted-basis, the same ratio stood at its early October 2021 level.
